coredata.dc:title: Facile pH-sensitive optical detection of pathogenic bacteria and cell imaging using multi-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Pathak, Abhishek
Venugopal, Parvathy
Nair, Bipin G.
Suneesh, Punathil Vasu
Satheesh Babu, T.G.
coredata.dc:description: Rapid detection of lethal pathogens is critical to abate the mortality rate of patients suffering from infectious diseases. Herein, we report a pH-sensitive detection of the pathogenic bacteria using multicolor emissive Nitrogen-doped Carbon Dots (NtCD) synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. The unique pH-sensitive interaction of NtCD with bacteria was best studied at pH 2. NtCD was competent to detect several pathogenic bacteria such as; Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) and Proteus vulgaris (P. vulgaris). The fluorescence spectral investigations of NtCD revealed the excitation dependent emission property with a red-shift. It shows an intense photoluminescent (PL) emission at λex/λem = 340/435 nm. The calculated quantum yield of NtCD was 27.2%. It also possesses multicolor emission at different excitation wavelengths. TEM analysis displayed the uniform spherical morphology of NtCD particles with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 3.11 ± 0.75 nm. The zeta potential of NtCD was measured as −8.4 mV at pH 2. The interaction between NtCD and bacteria was explained on the basis of their zeta potential values. The PL emission response of NtCD tagged bacteria was investigated at λex = 340 nm. It was potentially tagging to both E. coli and S. aureus but with a better response towards E. coli cells. The human squamous epithelial cells (SEC) were deployed as a fitting substitute for achieving NtCD-mammalian cell imaging. Both NtCD tagged bacterial cells and SEC exhibited multicolor emission as well. Mechanistic implications of pH-dependent PL emission property of NtCD and its bacterial interaction were discussed extensively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Pathogenic bacteria
Squamous epithelial cells
pH-sensitive
Nitrogen doping
Multicolor fluorimetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL emission variation of NtCD & NtCD tagged bacterial cells at various pH
Cytotoxicity assay of NtCD on bacterial cells
Fluorescence microscopic studies on NtCD tagged bacterial cells and human squamous epithelial cells (SEC)
Structural composition and morphological characterization of NtCD
Mechanistic implications to study PL emission behaviour of NtCD tagged bacterial cells with pH change
Multicolor imaging of NtCD tagged bacterial cells
Imaging of NtCD tagged squamous epithelial cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Biotechnology, Government of West Bengal
Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support is provided by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), Govt. of India project no. BT/PR20898/MED/29/1211/2017. The authors thank Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), CUSAT, Kerala for their generous help in HR-TEM imaging of NtCD. The authors extend thanks to Mr. Jayesh Vasudeva Adhikari (Amgen, California), Mr. Arunbabu A. V, Mr. Varadharaja Perumal (IISc, Bangalore), Dr. Girish C. M., Mr. Manu Krishnan K. (Amrita Centre for Nanosciences, Kochi) and Ms. Divya Nair (Amrita School of Biotechnology, Kollam) for their altruistic support in making the pictorial representation of the methodology used in this work, performing the XPS characterization of NtCD, providing useful insights on the characterization data analysis and giving us a platform to implement MTT assay.

The financial support is provided by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), Govt. of India project no. BT/PR20898/MED/29/1211/2017. The authors thank Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), CUSAT, Kerala for their generous help in HR-TEM imaging of NtCD. The authors extend thanks to Mr. Jayesh Vasudeva Adhikari (Amgen, California), Mr. Arunbabu A. V, Mr. Varadharaja Perumal (IISc, Bangalore), Dr. Girish C. M. Mr. Manu Krishnan K. (Amrita Centre for Nanosciences, Kochi) and Ms. Divya Nair (Amrita School of Biotechnology, Kollam) for their altruistic support in making the pictorial representation of the methodology used in this work, performing the XPS characterization of NtCD, providing useful insights on the characterization data analysis and giving us a platform to implement MTT assay.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption (blue color plot) and PL emission plot (red color) of NtCD. (Inset-Images of NtCD solution under day-light and UV light) (b) PL emission scans of NtCD at the different excitation wavelength (300–500 nm) (c) Effect of change in pH (1–11) on PL emission response of NtCD (d) FTIR spectrum of NtCD.

(a) HR-TEM images for morphological analysis of NtCD (Inset: shows typical fringe patterns for d-spacing measurement of NtCD and was measured as 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite, Scale bar − 2 nm). (b) Histogram exhibiting the average hydrodynamic diameter. The size of NtCD was measured as 3.11 ± 0.75 nm. (Scale bar – 20 nm) (c) XRD pattern shows the graphitic carbon nature of amorphous NtCD.

Fluorescence microscopic images of multicolor emissive NtCD tagged bacterial strains such as S. aureus, E. coli, B. subtilis and P. vulgaris cells (at pH 2) with blue, green and red emission at λex ~ 365, 470, and 545 nm (Scale bar: 10 µ).
Fluorescence microscopic images of human SEC tagged with multicolor emissive NtCD at λex ~ 365, 470, and 545 nm (Scale bar: 20 µ).

head.ce:title: Facile pH-sensitive optical detection of pathogenic bacteria and cell imaging using multi-emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amrita Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 641112, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amrita Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Sciences, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 641112, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Sciences, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amrita School of Biotechnology, Amritapuri, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 690525, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amrita School of Biotechnology, Amritapuri, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Amrita Biosensor Research Lab, Amrita School of Engineering Coimbatore, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, 641112, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid detection of lethal pathogens is critical to abate the mortality rate of patients suffering from infectious diseases. Herein, we report a pH-sensitive detection of the pathogenic bacteria using multicolor emissive Nitrogen-doped Carbon Dots (NtCD) synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. The unique pH-sensitive interaction of NtCD with bacteria was best studied at pH 2. NtCD was competent to detect several pathogenic bacteria such as; Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) and Proteus vulgaris (P. vulgaris). The fluorescence spectral investigations of NtCD revealed the excitation dependent emission property with a red-shift. It shows an intense photoluminescent (PL) emission at λex/λem = 340/435 nm. The calculated quantum yield of NtCD was 27.2%. It also possesses multicolor emission at different excitation wavelengths. TEM analysis displayed the uniform spherical morphology of NtCD particles with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 3.11 ± 0.75 nm. The zeta potential of NtCD was measured as −8.4 mV at pH 2. The interaction between NtCD and bacteria was explained on the basis of their zeta potential values. The PL emission response of NtCD tagged bacteria was investigated at λex = 340 nm. It was potentially tagging to both E. coli and S. aureus but with a better response towards E. coli cells. The human squamous epithelial cells (SEC) were deployed as a fitting substitute for achieving NtCD-mammalian cell imaging. Both NtCD tagged bacterial cells and SEC exhibited multicolor emission as well. Mechanistic implications of pH-dependent PL emission property of NtCD and its bacterial interaction were discussed extensively.

ce:section.ce:para: Infectious diseases are a major contributor to human mortality and pose a significant global threat to mankind [1]. The bacteria led to fatal infectious diseases caused by contaminated food or water is a persistent and alarming situation to address. Although developing countries are much seriously affected by such infectious diseases, developed countries also have their share as hospital-acquired or nosocomial infections and co-infections [2]. In particular, bacterial infections have become one of the leading causes of death in humans. The early diagnosis of pathogens can create a considerable impact on shrinking the mortality rate [3].

The conventional diagnostic techniques for bacteria detection in a clinical specimen include a time-consuming colony count assay [4] with subdued sensitivity and selectivity. Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) [5] and immunoassay such as ELISA [6] ameliorate sensitivity and generate results in a quick time. But the drawback such as non-specific amplification, tedious primer designing, and expensive reagents makes it difficult for the regular health management of critically-ill patients. The microscopic examinations of stained bacteria are also considered to be an effective diagnostic tool [7]. The traditional bacterial staining can be attained by using organic dyes or fluorescent stains [8]. The major limitations associated with the organic or fluorescent dyes for microscopic imaging of bacteria are low photostability, cytotoxicity, poor hydrophilicity, and rapid photobleaching [9].

Fluorescent nanoparticles (FNPs) are a promising alternative to harmful organic stains and are significant in medical imaging. It has also been explored for sensitive and selective bacterial detection. FNPs are virtually inert and do not change its intrinsic optical and electronic properties while interacting with a biological entity like bacteria. Till date, several attempts have been made to accomplish the bacterial detection such as E. coli using FNPs [10,11]. Nonetheless, the synthesis protocol of FNPs is complex, laborious, and involved multiple pathways to achieve the intended functionalized nanomaterial.

Among several subsets of FNPs, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have a facile synthesis method and are extensively deployed for bacterial sensing [12]. Fluorescent CDs are a budding but significant class of nanomaterial and generally defined by their characteristic sizes of less than 10 nm [13–16]. Its conventional synthesis strategies include laser ablation [17], plasma treatment [18], electrochemical synthesis [19], hydrothermal synthesis [20], and microwave-assisted synthesis [21,22]. CDs have been used as an intriguing tool in the development of fluorescent sensor platforms especially for bacterial detection and cell imaging [23,24]. This is because of its brilliant intrinsic properties such as low cytotoxicity, photo-resistant, tunable emission, compatibility with bio-molecules, controllable particle size, simple synthesis [25], and so forth. The accurate sensing of microbes such as Candida albicans [26], Caenorhabditis elegans [27] and E. coli like micro-organisms can be achieved by using CDs with improved Photo Luminescent (PL) emission property [28,29].

To enhance the fluorescence emission property of CDs, its chemical structure can be modified to regulate the opto-electronic properties [15]. This further intensifies the emission signal by ameliorating the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY). Similarly, heteroatom as a doping agent helps in incorporating the impurities into the carbogenic moiety of carbon dots. This significantly revamps the intrinsic properties of carbon dots and results in enhancing its PL emission intensity [30]. The dopants such as nitrogen, sulfur and manganese improves the PL emission intensity considerably for bacterial cell sensing and tissue imaging [31,32]. Nitrogen is considered as an ideal dopant to introduce the heteroatomic impurities into the carbon core and surface [33,34]. The nitrogen doping helps in increasing the electron density over the surface of carbon dots resulting in modulation of electronic and optical properties [30,35]. The long term stability of NtCD is due to the existence of a strong covalent bond between nitrogen and carbon. Also, the similarity exist between the atomic radius of carbon (0.77 Ǻ) and nitrogen (0.75 Ǻ) facilitates the fusion of N atoms into C matrix [36]. Since its inception, NtCD used for various applications such as detection of heavy metals (mercury [37] (Hg+2), chromium [38] (Cr+3), ferric ion [39] (Fe+3)), removal of organic and inorganic water pollutants [40], detection of antioxidant like glutathione [33], and mammalian cell imaging [41,42]. NtCD can be tuned as an effective sensing probe for the detection of an analyte or biological entities such as bacterial cells [43], mammalian cells [44] and many more. The most common properties that have been meticulously studied are quantum confinement, surface functional groups, and cumulative charge variation by adjusting the pH of NtCD solution. Wei and co-workers enhanced intrinsic PL emission of NtCD by reducing C=O bonds present over the surface of carbon dots to C-O bonds after treating with sodium borohydride [45] Hola et al. used nitrogen as a dopant to alter the surface negative charge causing red-shift of photoluminescence and resulted into red-emitting NtCD [46]. Kalaiyarasan group investigated the NtCD with improved electronic properties by photo activating the electron transfer process responsive to change in pH. The pH-sensitive NtCD has further been used for the estimation of Cu+2 and vitamin C [47].

In this article, pH-sensitive optical detection of pathogenic bacteria was achieved by using NtCD with its multicolor emission properties for cell imaging. NtCD was synthesized (as reported by Dong et al.) using a one-step hydrothermal method with known precursors namely; citric acid and glycine [20]. The electronic characterization of NtCD was carried out by analysing absorption and PL emission spectra. NtCD exhibits significant PLQY (27.2%). The surface topology was estimated by using High Resolution-Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM), Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) analysis. The measured size of NtCD was 3.11 ± 0.75 nm and the height profile was 3.35 ± 0.99 nm. The functional group estimation was carried out by Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR) and chemical composition analysis was performed by X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The net charges present on the surface of NtCD, NtCD tagged E. coli and S. aureus were analyzed by zeta potential (ζ) analyzer. NtCD stability was studied by exploring the PL emission response at different temperature, ionic conditions, time dependent fluorescence emission and reversibility of NtCD with pH change. The cell cytotoxicity of NtCD was investigated by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Additionally, to our previous work [28], the pH-sensitive pathogen detection as an application of NtCD was explored by tagging different bacteria such as E. coli, S. aureus, and so-forth at pH 2. Similarly, to explore the mammalian cell imaging potential of NtCD, SECs were employed as an ideal candidate. The NtCD tagged bacterial cells and SECs were found to emit multicolor emission at different excitation wavelengths. To the best of our knowledge, the pH-sensitive property of NtCD has never been attempted before for the detection of pathogenic bacteria. The holistic approach of pH-sensitive tagging of NtCD with pathogenic bacteria provides a better insight on the efficient detection of lethal microbes in limited space settings. The approach perhaps becomes a fitting substitute to the conventional, commercialized and harmful synthetic dyes for efficient cell imaging.

Citric acid (C6H8O7) and glycine (C2H5NO2) were procured from Finar. Peptone, yeast extract, sodium chloride, and agar powder were obtained from Himedia Pvt. Ltd. for the preparation of bacterial growth media. The reference fluorescent reagent, quinine sulfate, was also procured from Himedia Pvt. Ltd. The standard sterile conditions were maintained throughout the media preparation and microbial cell culture. To adjust the pH of the CDs solution and media, 1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl) solutions were purchased from Finar. The effect of ionic concentration on the stability of NtCD was studied by using sodium chloride (NaCl) and was procured from Finar. Ultrapure (MiliQ) water (18.2 MΩ.cm at 25 °C) was used for the experimental investigations. The pH meter was calibrated using different pH calibration buffers and was procured from Hanna instruments. MTT assay kit was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich.

The absorption spectrum was obtained by using a UV-1700 Pharmaspec, Shimadzu spectrophotometer. PL emission measurements were conducted by Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, Agilent, USA. The morphology and particle size estimation of NtCD was achieved by HR-TEM (JEM-2100, JEOL (Tokyo, Japan)) and DLS (Malvern Zetasizer ZSP). Height profiling of NtCD was attained by using AFM (Park systems- XE70, (Seoul, South Korea). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out with Rigaku Miniflex 600 diffractometer (Rigaku America, Woodlands, TX, USA). FTIR spectrum was scanned in the transmission mode on a Thermo Nicolet, iS10 FTIR spectrophotometer (Madison, WI USA). XPS analysis was performed using KRATOS Axis- Ultra, Schimadzu (Kratos Analytical Ltd., UK). The zeta potential analysis was carried out using Particle Analyzer (Litesizer 500, Anton Paar, Austria). The fluorescence microscope deployed in imaging NtCD tagged bacterial and mammalian cells was Carl Zeiss Axio Vert.A1 (Germany).

NtCD synthesis was carried out as per the reported procedure [20] with slight modification. In brief, NtCD preparation involved 2 g of citric acid and 0.62 g of glycine mixed thoroughly using mortar and pestle. This was finally dissolved in autoclaved Milli-Q water (5 mL). The solution was incubated in a hot air oven at 70 °C for 12 h. The thick syrup obtained after incubation was transferred into 100 mL Teflon based autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 230 °C for 6 h. The heating rate was 15 °C min−1. As the reaction completed, the temperature of the autoclave was slowly dropped to room temperature (RT). The resulting dark syrup after hydrothermal synthesis was diluted to 10 mL of Milli-Q water. The concentration of NtCD stock solution was 1.1 mg mL−1. The stock solution of NtCD was centrifuged at high speed (at 12000 rpm) for 60 mins to remove the unreacted impurities of NtCD solution in the form of a precipitate. The NtCD solution was further purified using a 0.22-µm syringe filter followed by dialysis (Molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the membrane = 1 kDa) for 48 h in Milli-Q water. NtCD solution was diluted to a pale yellow color solution (0.1 mg mL−1) for imaging bacterial cells.

To assess the QY of NtCD (ϕ NtCD ) at different pH, quinine sulfate in 0.5 M H2SO4 (quantum yield, 54%) was used as a reference fluorescence reagent. The final results were calculated according to Eq. (1).
(1)
Φ
NtCD
=
A b
std
Ab
NtCD
×
P L
NtCD
PL
std
×
n
2
NtCD
n
2
std
×
Φ
std

Φ std is the known QY of the standard quinine sulfate, PL NtCD and PL std stand for the integrated fluorescence emission intensities of NtCD and quinine sulfate (standard). A std and A NtCD are the absorbance values of the standard and NtCD at the λex = 340 nm, n 2
NtCD & n 2
std are the refractive index of NtCD in water (1.35) and quinine sulfate in sulfuric acid (1.37). The areas under the curve of integrated fluorescence emission intensities of NtCD solution at pH 1–11 were measured at λex = 340 nm. In this work, the calculated quantum yield of NtCD was 27.2%.

The different bacterial strains such as E. coli, S. aureus, P. vulgaris, and B. subtilis were opted for the experimental investigations. The laboratory glasswares used for bacterial cell culture were sterilized in an autoclave with standard procedures and conditions. The Luria-Bertani (LB) media (peptone, yeast extract, and sodium chloride prepared in Milli-Q water) was sterilized and used as a nutrient source for the growth of bacteria in the controlled environment. A single colony of each bacterial strain was inoculated in sterilized LB broth (5 mL) overnight in a shaker- incubator at 37 °C, 180 rpm. The growth of the bacteria culture was monitored until the absorbance at 600 nm (OD600) reached 0.5. The bacterial cells were pelleted (5500 rpm, 10 mins) and washed twice with 0.1 M PBS (Phosphate Buffer Saline). The washed cells were finally reconstituted in 1 mL solution of PBS. After washing, the bacterial cells were tagged with NtCD for cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL emission variation of NtCD & NtCD tagged bacterial cells at various pH
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission was scanned from 300 to 500 nm excitation wavelength to study the PL emission behaviour of NtCD. The disparity in PL emission of pH-sensitive NtCD was investigated from pH 1–11. The pH of NtCD stock solution was measured as pH 2. The precise changes in pH of NtCD solution were achieved by using standard solutions of a strong acid (1 M HCl) and base (1 M NaOH). The PL emission behaviour of NtCD solution was observed from pH 2 to 11. On a similar note, to study the PL emission response of pH-sensitive NtCD tagged bacterial cell, E. coli and S. aureus were deployed. The overnight grown bacterial culture was washed twice with PBS. The bacterial cell tagging assay was performed by deploying NtCD solution (0.1 mg mL−1) with pH ranging from 2 to 9. The washed cells were mixed gently with NtCD solution of different pH and incubated at RT for 15 mins. Post incubation, washing was done with PBS to remove the unbound NtCD. The PL intensity of pH-sensitive NtCD tagged bacteria cells were then measured at λex/λem = 340/435 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay of NtCD on bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para:
The MTT assay was attempted to investigate the cytotoxic nature of NtCD towards live E. coli and S. aureus cells. MTT or cytotoxicity assay is a colorimetric test for analyzing the cell viability of a living cell. The living cells are metabolically active and synthesize NAD(P)H-dependent cellular oxidoreductase enzymes in mitochondria. Moreover, in the presence of the tetrazolium dye MTT the enzymes of viable cells form a purple colored insoluble formazan product and help significantly in quantifying the number of living cells. In brief, 108 bacterial cells were incubated with different concentrations of NtCD (25, 100, 250, 450, and 850 µg mL−1) for 24 h. After the incubation, 100 µL of the sample was aliquot in triplicates and 10 µL of MTT (5 mg mL−1) was added in each well of the multi-well plate. The mixture was incubated for 4 h at 37 ˚C. The sufficient air was supplied and the medium was agitated on a gentle shaker. The solubilization solution (100 µL) was added to each well so that the insoluble formazan crystals can be solubilized. After 30 mins of incubation in shaker-incubator, the absorbance of the incubated samples present in multiple wells was measured at 570 nm in a plate reader (Synergy HT, BioTek). The percentage viability was calculated according to Eq. (2):
(2)
% v i a b i l i t y =
OD
CD
-
OD
blank
(
OD
control
-
OD
blank
)
× 100
where, OD CD is the optical density of bacterial cells treated with carbon dots at 570 nm, OD control is the optical density of untreated bacterial cells (i.e; without carbon dots) at 570 nm and OD blank is the optical density of media only (background) at 690 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence microscopic studies on NtCD tagged bacterial cells and human squamous epithelial cells (SEC)
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli and S. aureus cultures (OD600 ~ 0.5) were used for NtCD tagging experiment at pH 2. The washed bacterial cells were mixed with various concentrations of NtCD such as 0.025, 0.0325, 0.065, 0.1, 0.250, 0.450 and 0.850 mg mL−1 and incubated for 15 mins at RT. Post-incubation and washing, the NtCD tagged bacterial cells were imaged for morphological screening. Similarly, the tagging potential of NtCD (pH 2) was investigated on the mammalian SEC. The saliva sample was diluted immensely using PBS to reduce the viscous nature of the sample and centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 5 mins. The salivary cell pellet was dissolved in 0.1 M PBS. The mammalian SECs and NtCD were mixed (0.1 mg mL−1) in an equal volume ratio and incubated for 15 mins at RT. Again, after incubation and washing, the NtCD tagged SEC were imaged under fluorescence microscope with different excitation filters at 40X magnification [28]. It was concluded that the NtCD with 0.1 mg mL−1 concentration was the most suitable working concentration among all for tagging bacterial cells (results not shown).

The optical and electronic nature of water-soluble NtCD were examined at RT by using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometer. NtCD solution was transparent and pale yellow in daylight but under UV light, a bright blue color emission was observed (as shown in the inset of Fig. 1 (a)). At different pH, the NtCD solution did not show variation in color under daylight but in UV lamp the PL intensity change was significantly evident (Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM)-Fig. S7). The NtCD solution was homogeneous without any precipitation at RT. The absorption plot of NtCD showed two shoulder peaks. The first peak at 260 nm is assigned to π–π* transition of sp2 carbon domain. The second faint peak was at 340 nm wavelength is typically due to n–π* transition of CO bond. The fluorescence spectrum of NtCD showed maximum excitation (λex) and its corresponding emission wavelength (λem) at 340 and 435 nm, respectively (Fig. 1(a)). The calculated QY of NtCD at pH 2 was as high as 27.2%. Similarly, the QY of NtCD was calculated at various pH (precisely from 2 to 11) to check the stability of NtCD and discussed in ESM Section S6. The PL emissions scan at different excitation wavelengths (300 to 500 nm) displayed a red-shift while moving towards longer wavelengths (Fig. 1(b)). Hence, NtCD displayed excitation dependent PL emission as one of its intrinsic properties. The theoretical concepts behind the aforementioned property can be quantum confinement effect [48], surface traps model [49], or the electronegativity of heteroatoms [50–52]. Perhaps, the prime reason is the presence of different emissive sites with an abundance of functional groups such as CO, N–H, and O–H on the surface of the NtCD causing red-shift [53]. PL emission behaviour of NtCD solution was also screened at various protonic conditions (from pH 1 to 11) as exhibited in Fig. 1(c). The excitation wavelength was fixed at 340 nm. The significant variation in PL intensity was observed at discrete pH solutions of NtCD. PL intensity was highest at pH 2 (acidic environment) and lowest at pH 11 (alkaline environment). Apparently, the PL emission intensites at pH 1 and 2 were nearly identical. The reason could be the proton saturation of nitrogen sites in NtCD at pH 2 with no obvious possibility of extra proton attachment to the protonated NtCD nitrogen. Therefore, to achieve NtCD based bio-imaging applications, rest all the experiments were carried out at pH 2 (pH of stock solution). The variation in PL emission response can be described on the basis of pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen present on the surface of NtCD. The pyridinic nitrogen consists of lone pair of electrons on the outer side of pyridinic ring and exhibits significant non-radiative recombination to quench the fluorescence by photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process [54]. Therefore, at low pH, lone pair of electrons engages in the formation of protonation complexes resulting in blocking of PET pathway and thus facilitates the fluorescence enhancement through local emission states [55,56]. The stated reason has been validated by an excellent response of PL emission to changing pH values (Fig. 1(c)).

On the contrary, the radiative recombination process is evident in pyrrolic nitrogen due to the lone pair of electrons involved in maintaining the conjugation of the ring structure [55]. A significant upsurge in the fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 2. The gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity from pH 2 to 11 is due to the de-protonation of NtCD. The de-protonation allows the pyridinic nitrogen to regain the lone pair of electrons and activates the PET pathway leading to quenching the fluorescence intensity of NtCD [56]. Furthermore, the protonation/de-protonation of NtCD at different pH was cross-validated by the zeta potential measurements. Thus, pH 2 was used as the 'tagging-pH' of NtCD solution to carry out the pH-sensitive detection of pathogenic bacteria.

The optical stability of NtCD was also investigated at different temperatures and ionic concentrations (Fig. S1 A and B). The time-dependent fluorescence emission intensity of NtCD under continuous irradiation was also measured to screen the photo-stability of NtCD as shown in Fig. S1 C. The explanation is provided in ESM Section S1. In view of exploring the bio-imaging applications, the reversibility of pH changes in a biological system cannot be ignored [57,58]. Consequently, the reversibility of NtCD was investigated by monitoring the PL intensity change from pH 9 to pH 2 and again to pH 9 up to five cycles as shown in Fig. S1 D. It was observed that the variation in PL intensity was minimal and was quite stable after five cycles, representing the brilliant reversibility of NtCD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural composition and morphological characterization of NtCD
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectrum of NtCD is shown in Fig. 1(d) and typically used for analyzing the functional group available on the surface of synthesized NtCD. The FTIR spectrum of NtCD exhibits a wide peak at 3543 cm−1 and ascribed to stretching vibration of O–H group. The small peak at 2990 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of N–H and is primarily available due to the nitrogen doping provided by glycine. The absorption peak typically at 1695 cm−1 ascribed the stretching vibration of CO group of amides. The corresponding low intense peak observed at 1430 cm−1 is assigned to C-N group. The peak at 1236 cm−1 allocated to stretching vibration of C–O group. The availability of O–H and CO groups confirms the hydrophilicity of NtCD due to which it is highly soluble in water. The chemical composition was analysed by using XPS and a detailed explanation has been provided in ESM Section S5. The results of FTIR and XPS were found to be in complete agreement with each other. Hence, the presence of N dopant on the surface of NtCD was confirmed. The XRD pattern of the NtCD, as shown in Fig. 2 (c), exhibits a prominent diffraction peak at 2θ = 25°. This was assigned to the highly disordered carbon atoms and further confirms the presence of graphite-like carbon atoms arranged in stacks of disordered planes. Moreover, the XRD pattern reveals the amorphous characteristics of the turbostratic carbon phase [59].

The morphological characterization of NtCD was carried out by using HR-TEM (as shown in Fig. 2(a)). The micrograph of NtCD showed many uniforms, mono-dispersed, and well separated spherical carbon dots of size less than 5 nm. The TEM data revealed the fringe pattern (d-spacing of 0.21 nm (inset)), corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphite [60]. The size of sixty spherical carbon dots ranges from 1.20 to 4.62 nm with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 3.11 ± 0.75 nm as shown in Fig. 2(b). Perhaps the variation in NtCD size explains the reason for characteristic multicolor emission phenomena of NtCD. The height profile was measured by using AFM (Fig. S2) and a detailed explanation has been provided in ESM Section S2.

To validate the hydrodynamic diameter of NtCD at different pH, DLS analysis was carried out as shown in Fig. S6. The NtCD size variation was measured as 1.03, 3.01, 17, and 71.2 nm at pH 2, 7, 9, and 11 respectively. Therefore, DLS data was also in close agreement with TEM and AFM results in terms of size variation. This concludes the bright NtCD emission at lower pH was from monomeric particles. The particles exhibited the tendency of forming aggregates only at pH 9 and above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic implications to study PL emission behaviour of NtCD tagged bacterial cells with pH change
ce:section.ce:para: The variation in PL emission response of NtCD tagged bacteria cells were analyzed at different pH. The highest PL emission of the tagged bacterial cells was observed at pH 2. The intensity of PL emission at pH 2 for NtCD tagged E. coli cells was slightly greater than NtCD tagged S. aureus cells, respectively and it follows a similar trend at different pH as well. The reason could be the availability of NtCD in close proximity to the bacterial cells. The zeta potential of E. coli is more negative (−18.4 mV) as compared to S. aureus (−13.4 mV). Therefore, the protonated NtCD (ζ ~ −8.4 mV), at pH 2, shows higher electrostatic interaction with E. coli than S. aureus cells. The interaction results in the reduction of net charge available on NtCD-E. color mixture than NtCD-S. aureus mixture. This is because at pH 2, more protonated-NtCD molecules come closer to E. coli and the net charge reduces to ζ ~ −7.7 mV. The net negative charge is higher in the case of NtCD tagged S. aureus cells (−12.8 mV) which indicate the weaker electrostatic interaction as shown in Fig. S3. Hence, NtCD tagged E. coli cells were appeared to be distinct and brighter than NtCD tagged S. aureus cells. The detailed discussion on zeta potential analysis is available in ESM Section S3.

With the increase in pH from 2 to 7, the N-rich functional group present on the surface of NtCD gets de-protonated. This results in deprivation of electrostatic interaction between NtCD and bacteria. Therefore, the tagging efficiency of NtCD with bacteria cells was mitigated with subdued PL emission response when the pH changes from 2 to 7. Perhaps the suggested mechanism provides the conceptual apprehension of PL emission behavior of NtCD tagged E. coli and S. aureus cells at varying pH for detecting different types of microbes effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor imaging of NtCD tagged bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: The competent multicolor imaging property of NtCD was tested by tagging it with several bacterial strains such as S. aureus, E. coli, B. subtilis, and P. vulgaris at pH 2 (Fig. 3 ). The cytotoxicity/MTT assay of NtCD was also carried out to validate its biocompatibility towards bacterial cells and the explanation is provided in ESM Section S4.

After an incubation of 15 mins with NtCD, all the bacterial strains were imaged using fluorescence microscopy. To cut down the possibility of auto-fluorescence the untreated bacterial cells were used as a control as displayed in Fig. S8. NtCD treated bacterial cells with their overlay images have been shown in Fig. S9. The tagged bacterial cells were imaged under different excitation filters such as 365, 470 and 545 nm with their respective blue (425 nm), green (525 nm), and red color (605 nm) emission. Although the principle behind the multicolor emission has not been clearly demonstrated, the two comprehensively accepted mechanism models have been proposed. The first model explain about the usage of nitrogen as a dopant due to which surface defects are created on NtCD. It can be described on the basis of functional groups (like N–H, C=O, and O–H) availability on the surface of NtCD. This helps in creating additional energy levels in the form of emissive trap sites. The electronic transition varied significantly due to local emissive states with different bandgaps resulting in multicolor emission. The second model explain about the size variation of NtCD and ranges from 1.5 nm to 4.8 nm (HR-TEM & DLS analysis). The variation in size is directly related to an increase or decrease in the bandgap. As the bandgap increases, emission wavelength changes accordingly. Therefore, multicolor emission was observed at varying excitation wavelength [61,62]. Hence, NtCD could be an ideal candidate for real-time and long-term imaging applications.

Despite NtCD showcased the cell tagging and imaging potential of an entire range of pathogenic bacteria, it experienced a few limitations which should not be ignored. The illumination particularly at UV excitation exhibits interference from biological/clinical samples such as blood and urine, wastewater sample, and others. The biological samples have a strong background for both UV absorption and PL emission wavelength. A similar concept is realized for wastewater samples as well. The background fluorescence interferes with the illumination of UV excitation wavelength resulting in weakening of the PL emission signals. Due to the intrinsic multicolor emissive property of NtCD, the UV excitation can be switch over to blue and green excitation. This gives the remarkable flexibility to bacterial cell tagging and imaging at different excitation wavelengths. However, a stringent study is required to investigate the actions of interfering agents and to filter the background fluorescence to achieve the sensitive detection of lethal pathogens present in clinical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of NtCD tagged squamous epithelial cells
ce:section.ce:para: The NtCD tagged SEC was imaged using a fluorescence microscope under various excitation filters as shown in Fig. 4 . In this case, NtCD exhibits cell internalization or endocytosis due to higher cell permeability as compared to the bacterial cell. The dimensions of SEC are 30–40 folds higher than a bacterial cell with a larger nucleus to cytoplasmic ratio. The NtCD was also permeable to the nuclear membrane due to which it crosses the nuclear envelope and labels the available nucleic acid. The NtCD tagged nucleus of SEC was distinctly visible because of the bigger sized nucleus. The NtCD capability of tagging nucleus makes it a potent candidate for various investigations like nucleic acid (DNA/RNA) quantification, protein estimation, and many more. The multicolor imaging of NtCD tagged SECs was attained once the labeled cells were illuminated with light of multiple wavelengths such as 365, 470, and 545 nm. The bright blue, green, and red colors were emitted from the tagged cells and the multicolor images were acquired using a fluorescence microscope. Thus, NtCD may provide a significant option to the exorbitant fluorescent markers or dyes for probing mammalian cell lines such as cancer cells. With all these NtCD labeling capabilities, still there is a scope of investigation required for bio-imaging in clinical samples.

In summary, a simple pH-sensitive detection method of pathogenic bacteria was developed using multicolor emissive nitrogen doped carbon dots (NtCD). The PL emission stability of NtCD was probed at different temperature and ionic conditions, under continous UV irradiation and also at reversible pH change for five continuous cycles to justify its robust nature. The PL emission was also tested at varying pH (1–11) and concluded the strong and stable PL intensity at pH 2 with high quantum yield (27.2%). The cell cytotoxicity nature of NtCD was tested by performed the viability test on two strains of bacteria. NtCD exhibited the relatable biocompatible nature for cell tagging and imaging assays. The pathogenic bacteria such as E. coli and S. aureus were tagged with NtCD at various pH (2–9). It was found that the best 'tagging pH' was pH 2. The zeta potential measurement exhibited electrostatic interaction between NtCD and pathogenic bacteria and hence, facilitates the cell tagging assay. The unique multicolor emissive property was observed from NtCD tagged pathogenic bacteria and mammalian SEC for cell imaging. Hence, this work presents a new platform for detecting pathogenic bacteria and multicolor cell imaging using a pH-sensitive, photo-resistant, and biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots as a better prospective to harmful organic dyes with utmost commercial viability.

Abhishek Pathak: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Parvathy Venugopal: Investigation. Bipin G. Nair: Formal analysis, Resources. Punathil Vasu Suneesh: Visualization, Writing - reviewing & editing. Satheesh Babu T.G: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing - reviewing & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The financial support is provided by the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), Govt. of India project no. BT/PR20898/MED/29/1211/2017. The authors thank Sophisticated Analytical Instruments Facility (SAIF), CUSAT, Kerala for their generous help in HR-TEM imaging of NtCD. The authors extend thanks to Mr. Jayesh Vasudeva Adhikari (Amgen, California), Mr. Arunbabu A. V, Mr. Varadharaja Perumal (IISc, Bangalore), Dr. Girish C. M., Mr. Manu Krishnan K. (Amrita Centre for Nanosciences, Kochi) and Ms. Divya Nair (Amrita School of Biotechnology, Kollam) for their altruistic support in making the pictorial representation of the methodology used in this work, performing the XPS characterization of NtCD, providing useful insights on the characterization data analysis and giving us a platform to implement MTT assay.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105324.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, S.-B. Yu, J.-S. Wei, and H.-M. Xiong, Full-Color Light-Emitting Carbon Dots with a Surface-State-Controlled Luminescence Mechanism, ACS Nano 10 (2016) 484–491.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly luminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots as an effective multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Qian, J. Ma, X. Shan, H. Feng, L. Shao, and J. Chen, Highly Luminescent N-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots as an Effective Multifunctional Fluorescence Sensing Platform, Chem. Eur. J. 20 (2014) 2254–2263.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controlling speciation of nitrogen in nitrogen-doped carbon dots by ferric ion catalysis for enhancing fluorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Hu, L. Li, and W. J. Jin, Controlling speciation of nitrogen in nitrogen-doped carbon dots by ferric ion catalysis for enhancing fluorescence, Carbon 111 (2017) 133–141.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with pH-responsive fluorescence: a review on synthesis and cell biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ehtesabi, Z. Hallaji, S. Najafi Nobar, and Z. Bagheri, Carbon dots with pH-responsive fluorescence: a review on synthesis and cell biological applications, Microchim. Acta 187 (2020) 150.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A red emissive two-photon fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for intracellular ph detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ye, Y. Xiang, Q. Wang, Z. Li, and Z. Liu, A Red Emissive Two‐Photon Fluorescence Probe Based on Carbon Dots for Intracellular pH Detection, Small 15 (2019) 1901673.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots originated from carnation for fluorescent and colorimetric pH sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Zhong, H. Miao, K. Yang, and X. Yang, Carbon dots originated from carnation for fluorescent and colorimetric pH sensing, Mater. Lett. 166 (2016) 89–92.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blue light-emitting carbon dots (CDs) from a milk protein and their interaction with Spinacia oleracea leaf cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. K. Bajpai, A. D’Souza, and B. Suhail, Blue light-emitting carbon dots (CDs) from a milk protein and their interaction with Spinacia oleracea leaf cells, Int. Nano Lett. 9 (2019) 203–212.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Patir and S. K. Gogoi, Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as fluorescence ON–OFF–ON sensor for parallel detection of copper (ii) and mercury (ii) ions in solutions as well as in filter paper-based microfluidic device, Nanoscale Adv. 1 (2019) 592–601.
